runs.interpretation: Compensated substitution elasticity of labor supply for primary earners in the 6th earnings decile within a US tax-benefit microsimulation framework.
citations: Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. H. (2012). The elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates: A critical review.
Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions.
Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical literature on intensive-margin labor supply for primary earners (often men or heads of households) typically finds small positive elasticities. While the elasticity of taxable income can be higher due to avoidance, the substitution elasticity for hours worked in the middle of the distribution is consistently estimated to be low, generally between 0.05 and 0.30, with 0.15 serving as a standard consensus-adjacent central estimate.
runs.rawResponse:

  "interpretation": "Compensated substitution elasticity of labor supply for primary earners in the 6th earnings decile within a US tax-benefit microsimulation framework.",

    "p25": 0.1,
    "p50": 0.15,
    "p75": 0.25,
    "p95": 0.4
  },

    "Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. H. (2012). The elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates: A critical review.",
    "Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on elasticities with optimization frictions.",
    "Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical literature on intensive-margin labor supply for primary earners (often men or heads of households) typically finds small positive elasticities. While the elasticity of taxable income can be higher due to avoidance, the substitution elasticity for hours worked in the middle of the distribution is consistently estimated to be low, generally between 0.05 and 0.30, with 0.15 serving as a standard consensus-adjacent central estimate."
}

runs.interpretation: The compensated (Hicksian) wage elasticity of labor supply for primary earners in the 6th decile of the U.S. earnings distribution, used in tax-benefit microsimulation models to predict hours/participation response.
citations: Saez, E. (2002). Optimal Income Transfer Programs: Intensive versus Extensive Labor Supply Responses.
Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions.
Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches.
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical labor supply literature generally suggests primary earners (especially men) have very inelastic labor supply responses to wages. While secondary earners and those at the extensive margin (participation) show higher elasticities, primary earners in the middle deciles of the distribution are typically modeled with small positive substitution elasticities. A value of 0.1 to 0.2 is the standard calibration range in microsimulation models like TAXSIM or CBO models.

While secondary earners and those at the extensive margin (participation) show higher elasticities, primary earners in the middle deciles of the distribution are typically modeled with small positive substitution elasticities. A value of 0.1 to 0.2 is the standard calibration range in microsimulation models like TAXSIM or CBO models."
